questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with acute decompensated heart failure who has a new order for furosemide 40 mg IV push. Which assessment finding is MOST important to report to the provider before administering this medication?

options.text: The patient's weight is 4 lbs above their dry weight
The patient's serum potassium is 3.0 mEq/L
The patient has bilateral 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities
The patient reports urinary urgency

questions.rationale: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that causes potassium wasting. A pre-dose potassium of 3.0 mEq/L (hypokalemia) is a critical safety concern — administering furosemide will further lower potassium, increasing the risk of life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias (torsades de pointes, ventricular fibrillation), especially in heart failure patients who may be on digoxin. Potassium replacement should be confirmed or ordered before or concurrent with diuresis. Weight gain above dry weight and bilateral edema are the indications FOR furosemide, not contraindications. Urinary urgency is an expected diuretic side effect.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Before furosemide: check K+. If < 3.5 mEq/L, notify provider and hold or replace K+ first. Diuretics + hypokalemia = arrhythmia risk. This is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.

visualRationale.title: Cardiovascular / Heart Failure
visualRationale.caption: Before furosemide: check K+. If < 3.5 mEq/L, notify provider and hold or replace K+ first. Diuretics + hypokalemia = arrhythmia risk. This is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.
visualRationale.conclusion: Before furosemide: check K+. If < 3.5 mEq/L, notify provider and hold or replace K+ first. Diuretics + hypokalemia = arrhythmia risk. This is especially critical in patients on digoxin or with baseline dysrhythmias.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is admitted with RSV bronchiolitis. SpO2 is 91% on room air, RR 58, mild intercostal retractions, poor feeding. The physician orders albuterol. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options.text: Administer albuterol as ordered; it is the standard of care for RSV bronchiolitis
Question the order; bronchodilators are not recommended as routine treatment for bronchiolitis — prioritize nasal suctioning and supportive care
Hold the albuterol and administer corticosteroids instead
Administer albuterol only if SpO2 drops below 88%

questions.rationale: RSV bronchiolitis is managed with supportive care only per AAP guidelines: nasal suctioning (most effective intervention for symptom relief), adequate hydration (NG feeds if needed), and supplemental O2 for SpO2 <90–92%. Bronchodilators (albuterol) and corticosteroids are NOT recommended because bronchiolitis involves viral-induced inflammation and mucus plugging — not bronchospasm — making bronchodilators ineffective. Multiple RCTs have shown no benefit. The nurse should question the order and clarify before administration. Contact precautions (RSV is spread via droplet/contact) are required.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.
visualRationale.conclusion: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.

questions.stem: A patient arrives in the PACU after 2.5-hour general anesthesia for abdominal surgery. SpO2 is 88%, respiratory rate is 5 breaths/min and shallow, the patient is unresponsive to voice, and ETCO2 is 72 mmHg. What is the priority nursing action?

options.text: Verbally and physically stimulate the patient repeatedly and wait 5 minutes for residual anesthesia to clear
Elevate HOB to 30° and apply supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula at 4 L/min
Open the airway with a jaw thrust, assist ventilation with 100% O2 via bag-valve-mask, prepare reversal agents (naloxone or sugammadex as appropriate), and prepare for reintubation if no improvement
Notify the surgeon and anesthesiologist and await orders before intervening

questions.rationale: Post-anesthesia respiratory depression is a PACU emergency. SpO2 88% + RR 5 + unresponsive + ETCO2 72 mmHg = severe hypoventilation with CO2 retention — caused by residual opioids, residual neuromuscular blockade, or residual volatile agents. This requires IMMEDIATE active intervention: (1) Airway — jaw thrust to relieve obstruction in the obtunded patient; (2) Breathing — 100% O2 via bag-valve-mask with assisted ventilation (nasal cannula at 4 L/min provides approximately 36% FiO2 and no ventilatory support — completely inadequate); (3) Reversal agents — naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV for opioid-induced respiratory depression (titrate slowly to avoid acute agitation and pain); sugammadex 200 mg (or 16 mg/kg) for rocuronium/vecuronium reversal; flumazenil for benzodiazepines; (4) Prepare for reintubation if no response. The PACU nurse must act — immediate physician notification is also required, but waiting for orders before airway management is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: PACU respiratory emergency: SpO2 low + RR <8 + unresponsive + high ETCO2 = hypoventilation. Jaw thrust + BVM + 100% O2 immediately. Consider reversal agents (naloxone, sugammadex). Prepare to reintubate.

questions.stem: A nurse is providing discharge teaching to a patient newly prescribed levothyroxine (Synthroid) for hypothyroidism. Which instruction is MOST important to include?

options.text: Take the medication with a full meal to improve absorption
Take the medication on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast with water, and avoid taking it with calcium or iron supplements
Double the dose if a dose is missed to maintain thyroid hormone levels
Expect to see symptom improvement within 24-48 hours of starting therapy

questions.rationale: Levothyroxine absorption is significantly reduced by food, calcium, iron supplements, antacids containing calcium/magnesium, and several other medications. The medication should be taken on an empty stomach (30-60 minutes before breakfast) with a full glass of water. Calcium and iron supplements should be taken at least 4 hours apart from levothyroxine. Doubling a missed dose is incorrect and dangerous — skip the missed dose and continue at the regular schedule. Symptom improvement takes weeks to months (TSH normalization is checked at 4-6 weeks after dose adjustment), not 24-48 hours.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Levothyroxine: empty stomach + water + 30-60 min before breakfast. Separate from calcium, iron, antacids by 4h. Never double dose. Onset of therapeutic effect: 4-6 weeks for TSH normalization. Compliance is key — once-daily dosing forever.

questions.stem: A registered nurse is delegating morning care tasks to a nursing assistant (CNA). Which task is MOST appropriate to delegate?

options.text: Assess a new patient's skin for pressure injuries during the bed bath
Provide oral care to an unconscious patient on a ventilator
Instruct a patient with newly diagnosed diabetes about foot care
Measure and record vital signs for a stable post-operative patient on day 3

questions.rationale: Measuring and recording vital signs for a stable patient is a routine task within the CNA's scope of practice and does not require nursing judgment for data collection (though the RN interprets the results). Skin assessment requires RN-level assessment skills. Oral care for an unconscious, ventilated patient involves airway risk management and should be performed by licensed nursing staff. Patient education—such as foot care for a diabetic—is an RN responsibility that cannot be delegated.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Delegate tasks that are routine, do not require nursing judgment, and involve stable patients. Assessment, education, and care requiring clinical judgment remain with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient's pulmonary artery catheter readings show: PA pressure 42/22 mmHg, PCWP 24 mmHg, CVP 14 mmHg, and cardiac output 3.2 L/min. The nurse correctly interprets these findings as indicative of:

options.text: Right ventricular failure with fluid overload
Left ventricular failure with elevated filling pressures
Hypovolemia with compensatory tachycardia
Normal hemodynamic values within reference ranges

questions.rationale: Normal PCWP is 6–12 mmHg. A wedge pressure of 24 mmHg (>18 mmHg) indicates elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, consistent with left ventricular failure or cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The elevated PA pressures reflect backward transmission of elevated LV pressures. Low cardiac output (normal 4–8 L/min) further confirms reduced LV performance. CVP of 14 mmHg (normal 2–8 mmHg) reflects right heart congestion secondary to LV failure.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: PCWP >18 mmHg = LV failure / cardiogenic pulmonary edema. PCWP <6 mmHg = hypovolemia.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: Patient A (83 yo) has sudden-onset inattention, waxing-and-waning confusion, and visual hallucinations appearing 2 days after starting opioids post-hip surgery. Patient B (79 yo) has 4 years of progressive memory loss, gets lost in familiar environments, and repeatedly asks the same questions. Which statement correctly classifies these presentations?

options.text: Both have dementia; Patient A has early-onset and Patient B has late-stage Alzheimer's disease
Patient A has delirium (acute, fluctuating, reversible, identifiable cause); Patient B has dementia (insidious, progressive, irreversible)
Patient A has depression (pseudodementia) from opioid use; Patient B has Lewy body dementia
Both require CT head imaging to differentiate — clinical assessment alone is insufficient

questions.rationale: Delirium vs Dementia: Delirium — ACUTE onset (hours to days), FLUCTUATING course, typically reversible with treatment of precipitant (opioids, infection, metabolic derangement, post-op state), hallucinations (especially visual) common, INATTENTION is the cardinal distinguishing feature. Dementia — INSIDIOUS onset (months to years), PROGRESSIVE course, irreversible underlying pathology, recent memory loss predominates, attention relatively preserved until late stages. Patient A has delirium (acute, precipitant-identified — opioids) and Patient B has dementia (gradual progression). Treatment of delirium: remove or reduce offending drug, provide reorientation, preserve sleep-wake cycle, family presence, early mobility; low-dose haloperidol for severe agitation. Delirium in hospitalized elderly is associated with significantly increased mortality and prolonged hospitalization. Use CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) to screen.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Delirium: acute onset, fluctuating, reversible, identifiable cause, inattention is cardinal. Dementia: insidious, progressive, irreversible. Delirium and dementia can coexist — dementia is the #1 risk factor for delirium.

questions.stem: A patient who has been on chlorpromazine for 10 years develops involuntary, repetitive tongue protrusions, lip-smacking movements, and facial grimacing that persist even when the medication is held. Which condition does this describe?

options.text: Acute dystonia
Tardive dyskinesia
Akathisia
Pseudoparkinsonism

questions.rationale: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) develops after months to years of antipsychotic use. It is characterized by involuntary, repetitive, purposeless movements of the face, tongue, and extremities (lip smacking, tongue protrusion, choreiform movements). Key feature: movements PERSIST or worsen when the antipsychotic is reduced/stopped initially. It may be irreversible. VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine) are FDA-approved treatments. Acute dystonia (A) is sudden onset within days and responds to anticholinergics. Akathisia (C) is restlessness and urge to move. Pseudoparkinsonism (D) mimics Parkinson's disease.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Tardive dyskinesia: involuntary repetitive orofacial movements after years of antipsychotic use — may be irreversible; treat with VMAT2 inhibitors.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with severe asthma is admitted with an acute exacerbation. The nurse notes the patient is no longer wheezing despite having difficulty breathing and accessory muscle use. Which interpretation is CORRECT?

options.text: The patient is improving — decreased wheezing indicates reduced bronchospasm
The patient is deteriorating — absence of wheezing with severe work of breathing indicates 'silent chest,' signaling impending respiratory failure
The patient requires a bronchodilator since the wheezing has resolved
The patient is having an allergic reaction rather than an asthma attack

questions.rationale: A 'silent chest' in an asthmatic patient with severe respiratory distress is an ominous sign — it does not indicate improvement. It indicates airways are so severely obstructed that no air movement is occurring, thus no wheeze can be generated. This is a pre-arrest condition requiring immediate escalation: notify provider, prepare for intubation, consider IV magnesium sulfate, IV epinephrine or terbutaline, and high-dose continuous bronchodilators. The paradox of asthma: the sicker the patient, the less they wheeze because they lack airflow. Wheezing returning with treatment is actually a sign of improvement.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Silent chest in asthma = no airflow = impending respiratory arrest. This is a critical sign, NOT improvement. Escalate immediately. Wheezing on treatment = airways opening = improvement.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Asthma Exacerbation
visualRationale.caption: Silent chest in asthma = no airflow = impending respiratory arrest. This is a critical sign, NOT improvement. Escalate immediately. Wheezing on treatment = airways opening = improvement.
visualRationale.conclusion: Silent chest in asthma = no airflow = impending respiratory arrest. This is a critical sign, NOT improvement. Escalate immediately. Wheezing on treatment = airways opening = improvement.

questions.stem: A patient is being discharged on prednisone 40 mg daily for 10 days following an acute COPD exacerbation. Which statement by the patient indicates that ADDITIONAL teaching is needed?

options.text: 'I should not stop this medication suddenly without talking to my doctor.'
'I can stop taking this medication early if I start feeling better.'
'I should monitor my blood sugar more closely while taking this medication.'
'I should take this medication in the morning with food.'

questions.rationale: Stopping corticosteroids abruptly or early is dangerous and incorrect. Even a 10-day course suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and requires a tapering strategy if used longer, and should always be completed as prescribed for a short course. Early discontinuation risks adrenal insufficiency and relapse of the condition being treated. The patient should understand that the full course must be completed, and that stopping suddenly (in longer courses) can cause an adrenal crisis. Statement A is correct — do not stop without medical advice. Statement C is correct — steroids cause hyperglycemia. Statement D is correct — morning administration mimics cortisol's natural diurnal rhythm and reduces GI upset.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Corticosteroid teaching: complete the full course, do not stop early or abruptly. Monitor blood glucose. Take in morning with food. Know signs of infection (immunosuppression). Report mood changes, edema, GI symptoms.
